prompt:
Infra ticket. Our deployment tooling shells out to `az` and pretends every ARM PUT is synchronous — it fires the request, sleeps 90 seconds, and hopes. Twice this month it declared render-pool rollouts "done" while the operation was still running, and once it looped forever on an operation that had actually Failed. I want a real C# client for this: a small library that submits an ARM create-or-update and drives the documented asynchronous-operation protocol to a final answer. The xunit test project (ArmLro.csproj + ArmLroTests.cs) is already in the repo and is the acceptance contract.

Do the research first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Microsoft docs — at minimum the Azure Resource Manager asynchronous-operations tracking page (status codes, Azure-AsyncOperation vs Location, Retry-After, the status-monitor body) and the Microsoft identity platform client-credentials flow page (token endpoint, form fields, the .default scope, the token response). The Azure REST API reference overview and the Azure REST API guidelines (for Operation-Location) are also first-party. Only Microsoft's own pages count as sources — no blogs, no Stack Overflow. Our pinned wire contract lives in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected fixtures. Compare the live docs against them — if anything disagrees, say so explicitly in your summary and leave the fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

What the tests expect (namespace ArmLro; split the implementation across a few sensible files — token acquisition, the LRO client/poller, models, and exceptions should not be one blob):

- EntraTokenProvider(HttpClient, Uri tokenEndpoint, string clientId, string clientSecret, string scope, Func<DateTimeOffset> now) with Task<string> GetTokenAsync(CancellationToken = default). It must POST the documented form, cache the token until expires_in runs out (use the injected clock — no DateTime.Now anywhere), refresh after expiry, and on a token error throw ArmAuthException whose message carries the OAuth error code but never the client secret.
- ArmLroClientOptions { int MaxPolls, TimeSpan DefaultPollDelay, Func<TimeSpan, CancellationToken, Task> Delay } — all waiting goes through the injected Delay; nothing in the library may actually sleep.
- ArmLroClient(HttpClient, EntraTokenProvider, ArmLroClientOptions) with Task<ArmOperationResult> CreateOrUpdateAsync(string resourcePath, string apiVersion, string requestBodyJson, CancellationToken = default). The PUT goes to the HttpClient's base address + resourcePath with the api-version query parameter, a Bearer token, and an application/json body.
- Follow the researched monitor-header precedence exactly, honor Retry-After between polls (falling back to DefaultPollDelay), understand what a Location-style monitor answers versus an Azure-AsyncOperation-style one, fetch the final resource where the docs say it lives, and stop on the documented terminal states (match them case-insensitively — providers are sloppy). Failed/Canceled come back as ArmOperationResult { Succeeded=false, Status, Error{Code,Message} }; success carries the final resource JSON. A non-LRO rejection (e.g. 400) throws ArmRequestException with the parsed ARM error body; protocol violations (202 with no monitor header, monitor body without status, poll budget exhausted) throw ArmProtocolException. One more thing I care about after last year's incident: if a monitor header ever points off the ARM host, do not send our bearer token there — refuse loudly.

Acceptance: dotnet test — everything green. ArmLroTests.cs, ArmLro.csproj, .gitignore and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: ARM REST API (management.azure.com) with Microsoft Entra OAuth 2.0 v2 client-credentials flow; asynchronous-operation tracking per the ARM async-operations contract

official_sources.title: Track asynchronous Azure operations - Azure Resource Manager
official_sources.used_for: 201/202 initial status codes, Azure-AsyncOperation preferred over Location for the status monitor URL, Retry-After as seconds between polls, status monitor body with status Succeeded/Failed/Canceled plus error{code,message} on failure, provisioningState terminal values, Location-URL monitors answering 202 while running and 200 with the final body when done, and the 4 KB minimum monitor-URL size

official_sources.title: Azure REST API reference documentation
official_sources.used_for: management.azure.com base host, the mandatory api-version query parameter on ARM requests, the Authorization Bearer header, Content-Type application/json for request bodies, and the async/throttling/paging pointers

official_sources.title: OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow on the Microsoft identity platform
official_sources.used_for: token endpoint https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant}/oauth2/v2.0/token, application/x-www-form-urlencoded POST with grant_type=client_credentials, client_id, client_secret, and scope={resource}/.default, the token_type/expires_in/access_token success body, and the error/error_description failure body

official_sources.title: Microsoft Azure REST API Guidelines
official_sources.used_for: the Operation-Location status-monitor header used by newer Azure services, retry-after expressed as an integer number of seconds, terminal status values Succeeded/Failed/Canceled, and the error body rule that error.code matches the x-ms-error-code header

research.language_rationale: The ARM long-running-operation contract is defined at the REST level (response headers and status-monitor JSON), and Azure's own .NET SDK implements exactly this header state machine inside Azure.Core. Building the poller on System.Net.Http.HttpClient from the BCL is the documented raw integration surface, keeps the seed dependency-free beyond xunit, and makes the researched precedence/Retry-After rules the actual deliverable instead of hiding them behind an SDK poller.
